answer:
In *Manusmṛti* Chapter 12, *guṇa* (guna) refers to the three fundamental constituent qualities or modes of nature (*sattva*, *rajas*, and *tamas*) that inhere in the Self (*Ātman*). These are the means by which the Great One (the supreme principle pervading reality) abides in and influences all beings, determining their predominant character and post-mortem rebirths.[^1]

Whichever *guṇa* predominates in an embodied being shapes its nature: *sattva* (purity/goodness) is equated with knowledge, associated with pleasure, tranquility, Vedic study, *tapas* (austerity), sense-control, and *dharma*; *rajas* (passion/activity) with aversion, pain, desire for action/fame, impatience, and sense-indulgence; *tamas* (inertia/darkness) with ignorance, delusion, greed, cruelty, sleep, and carelessness.[^2][^3] Actions aligned with *tamas* are shameful; those with *rajas* seek worldly gain; those with *sattva* align with *dharma*.[^4]

Beings dominated by *sāttvika* qualities are reborn in higher divine states (e.g., gods, sages, Brahma); *rājasic* in human or semi-divine forms (e.g., kings, Gandharvas); *tāmasic* in lower animal or demonic ones (e.g., beasts, worms, Rakshasas).[^5]

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 12, paragraph p-074f6e](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-074f6e).
[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 12, paragraph p-074f6e](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-074f6e) (esp. Mn_12.24–29).
[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 12, paragraph p-074f6e](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-074f6e) (esp. Mn_12.31–34).
[^4]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 12, paragraph p-074f6e](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-074f6e) (esp. Mn_12.35–38).
[^5]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 12](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/) (esp. Mn_12.39–51).

args.query: "three qualities" OR "three gunas" OR sattva OR rajas OR tamas OR "mode of nature" Manusmriti
sattva OR rajas OR tamas chapter 12
